title: Shortbread
subtitle: Crisp at the edges, soft in the middle.
excerpt: Shortbread. A baking recipe from Rebecca's collection, straightforward, reliable, worth weighing rather than scooping.
sourceNotes: From Rebecca's personal cookbook. Bread recipes draw on the long tradition of home baking, Elizabeth David's English Bread and Yeast Cookery is one of the canonical references in English.

content.text: Shortbread. A British home-cook's dish, the kind of recipe families adjust across generations. Adjustable for what you need.
Cookies bake fast and reward a chilled dough. Pull them out when the edges set and the middles still look slightly underdone.

Start by creaming together the butter and sugar until pale and creamy. For a simple recipe like this that doesn’t call for additional flavourings or salt, be sure to use salted butter to bump up the flavour.

After you’ve creamed your butter and sugar, add in 3-3/4 cups of flour and mix until combined. Then turn the dough out onto a floured surface and add in the remaining flour until you have a soft dough.

Lightly flour a work surface, and roll out the dough until it’s about a half inch thick. Cut into 3×1-inch strips. You can use a knife, a pizza cutter or a bench scraper to do this. Place about an inch apart on an ungreased cookie sheet and prick with a fork to get that signature Scottish shortbread cookie design.

After shaping the cookies, don’t rush to the oven! Instead, chill the dough in the refrigerator for 30 minutes or so (overnight is OK, too). A short stay in the fridge will firm up the cookies and solidify the butter. This will help keep them from spreading too much. Also, it helps concentrate those flavours for your best ever shortbread.
Bake in a 325ºF oven for 20 to 25 minutes until the edges are lightly browned. Let the cookies cool on the pan for about five minutes before transferring to a wire rack to cool completely.

attrs.intro: A few of the more common issues, and what tends to fix them.

items.fix: Chill the dough for 30 minutes before baking. Preheat the oven properly, 180°C means 180°C on an oven thermometer, not the dial.
items.cause: Butter too soft, or oven not hot enough.
items.symptom: Cookies spread too thin and crisp

items.fix: Stop mixing as soon as the flour disappears. For chewier cookies, replace some plain flour with bread flour for more gluten.
items.cause: Over-mixed once the flour went in, or too much flour.
items.symptom: Cookies came out cakey, not chewy

items.fix: Leave them on the tray for five minutes after baking. They're too soft to move when hot. Lift onto a rack with a palette knife.
items.cause: Tried to lift before they'd set.

items.fix: Use a heavier baking tray, line with baking paper, and bake on the middle shelf.
items.cause: Tray too thin, or rack too low.

attrs.heading: If something goes wrong

content.text: Add-ins: 75 g dark chocolate chips, chopped walnuts, dried cranberries, or a pinch of sea salt on top before baking.
Gluten-free: replace plain flour with a 1-to-1 gluten-free blend that includes xanthan gum.
Best fresh. Leftovers keep two days in the fridge; reheat covered with a splash of liquid.
A British home cook's dish. It sits among the recipes families remember without thinking about, the kind that gets adjusted across generations and never quite the same twice.
